hero.title: FitGardener

hero.tagline: Growing strength, growing greens — one bed at a time.

about.title: About Dan
about.bio: Gardening enthusiast and fitness junkie. I started FitGardener to document my journey building a productive garden while staying active and healthy. No fancy degrees — just dirty hands, hard work, and a passion for growing my own food.

garden.title: The Garden
garden.description: A work in progress. Each season I expand the plot, improve the soil, and try something new.
garden.highlight: Tomatoes, zucchini, kale, herbs, and strawberries every summer.

contact.title: Get in Touch
contact.note: Have a question about gardening in cold climates? Send it over — I reply when I can.

posts.title: Spring Planting 2026 — What's Going In
posts.excerpt: Another year, another shot at the perfect garden. Here's the plan for this season.
posts.content:
Spring is finally here and the soil is warming up enough to work with. This year I'm trying a few new things: a potato patch in raised beds, more herbs along the south side of the house, and a cold frame for early season greens...

The compost is ready to work in. Last year's kitchen scraps broke down beautifully. Time to give back to the beds.

posts.title: Starting Seeds Indoors — What Actually Works
posts.excerpt: After three years of trial and error, here are the only tips you actually need.
posts.content:
Seed starting doesn't need to be complicated. But most guides make it sound like you need a whole grow room setup. You don't.

I start everything under a $40 grow light from Amazon, in solo cups with drainage holes, on a wire rack over a heater vent. That's it. No fancy humidity domes, no heating mats except for peppers...

The biggest lesson: less is more. Don't overwater. Don't overfeed. Give them light and walk away.

posts.title: What I Learned From Last Year's Garden
posts.excerpt: The 2025 season was a mixed bag. Here's the honest recap.
posts.content:
2025 was my most ambitious garden yet — 6 raised beds, a new compost bin, and my first real attempt at winter gardening with a cold frame.

The cold frame failed. The tomatoes were incredible. The zucchini overwhelmed everyone I knew. The kale kept producing into November.

Biggest win: no-dig beds. I layered cardboard, compost, and straw in the fall. Spring soil was dark, crumbly, and ready to plant with zero digging.

Biggest fail: starting too many pepper varieties without labeling them. I still don't know what half my pepper plants were.
